full_text: Today we look at the question of wealth. Okay. The question is why do poor people misunderstand wealth and why does this misunderstanding keep them poor? Okay. So we we've done a lot of research and we've discovered that people who who understand wealth um understand it for certain reasons. Those who don't understand it often think they do. That's what what's called survivorship bias. And this is this helps explain why financial advice doesn't work because we only study rich people who succeeded. We ignore all the rich people who failed. Okay? Like for every Warren Buffett, there's a thousand people who invested the same way and went bankrupt. But we don't write books about them. So all our financial advice comes from survivors. And survivor advice doesn't work for people who aren't survivors yet. Okay. Okay. You guys, you guys need to remember this. Just because rich people do certain things does not mean doing those things makes you rich. So, I'll give you an example. We know that wealthy people, they invest in the stock market. Okay? They have diversified portfolios. But just because you invest in the stock market does not mean you'll become wealthy. Okay? Just because you read financial books does not mean you'll build wealth. But if you're wealthy, you will invest in the stock market because you have money you don't need for survival. Okay, does that make sense? So if you're rich, guess what happens? You become wealthy and you'll understand compound interest. Okay, does that make sense? Okay, so the question for us is why does this happen? And ultimately, what's the difference between being rich and understanding wealth? Okay. So, we know for a fact that people who understand wealth are much more likely to build generational wealth than people who don't understand it. And in fact, what we know from economic studies is that financial literacy doesn't really matter. Doesn't matter how much you know about money. If you don't understand wealth, you'll stay poor. If you understand wealth, you'll build generational fortune. Okay? So, what's the difference? Okay. So now let's look at the fundamental differences between how poor people think about money and how rich people think about wealth. Okay. So there are lots of differences but let's focus on three major differences. The first major difference is what I call time horizon blindness. Okay. So poor people when they think about wealth they think wealth is about having money now. right now today. Okay. If you have $10,000 in your bank account, a poor person thinks I am rich. Okay? But if you're a rich person and you have $10,000 in your bank account, you think this is nothing. This is basically zero. Does that make sense? So here's the key difference. Poor people see wealth as an amount, a number. I have X dollars, therefore I'm wealthy. But rich people see wealth as independence from labor. Okay? So when a rich person says, "I'm wealthy," they don't mean, "I have a lot of money." They mean, "I never have to work again and my lifestyle doesn't change." Okay? So a poor person with $10,000 thinks they're temporarily rich. A rich person with $10,000 thinks they're basically broke because that$10,000 doesn't buy them time. Does that make sense? And this goes back to time horizons. Poor people think about money in terms of weeks or months. Can I pay rent this month? Can I buy groceries this week? Okay. Rich people think about money in terms of forever. Will this asset generate income for my grandchildren? Okay. So, poor people optimize for having money now. Rich people optimize for controlling time forever. And because they're thinking on completely different time scales, they make completely different decisions. A poor person saves money in a bank account because they might need it next month. A rich person would never save money in a bank account because that money is not working for them. It's not buying them more time. Okay. The second major difference is what I call asset versus income confusion. So if you think that if you have a high salary, you're wealthy, right? Like a doctor making $300,000 a year. Poor people look at that and think, "Wow, that doctor is rich." Okay? But rich people look at that same doctor and think that doctor is a highincome worker, but they're not wealthy. Does that make sense? Here's why. The doctor makes $300,000 a year, but they have to work to make that money. If the doctor stops working, the money stops. Okay? So, the doctor has a high income, but they're not wealthy. Now, compare that to a landlord who owns apartment buildings that generate $100,000 a year in rent. The landlord makes less money than the doctor, but the landlord is wealthier. Why? because the landlord doesn't have to work for that h 100,000. The buildings generate money while the landlord sleeps. Okay, so here's the key misunderstanding. Poor people optimize for income. They want a higher salary, a better paying job, a promotion, a raise. Rich people optimize for equity and ownership. They want things that generate money without their labor. Okay? So poor people save cash in a bank. Rich people buy things that make more money. Poor people work for money. Rich people make money work for them. Does that make sense? And this is why when you tell a poor person, you should invest, they don't really understand what that means. To them, investing means putting money somewhere they can't touch it. It feels like losing money. But to a rich person, investing means buying something that will generate more money. It feels like planting seeds. Okay. completely different mindset. The poor person sees an expense. The rich person sees an asset. Okay. The third major difference is what I call risk perception inversion. Okay. Risk perception. So poor people think that having a job is safe and starting a business is risky. Right? Everyone knows this. Get a good job, that's the safe path. starting a business that's risky. You might lose everything. Okay? But rich people think the exact opposite. Rich people think depending on one income source is risky. Having only a job is dangerous, but having multiple income streams, that's safe. Okay? So, let me give you an example. A poor person has a job that pays $50,000 a year. They're terrified of quitting because if they quit, they have zero income. So, they stay at the job even if they hate it, even if it's going nowhere because the alternative is no income. That feels risky. Okay. But a rich person looks at that same situation and thinks, "Oh my god, you only have one income source. What if you get fired? What if the company goes bankrupt? What if you get sick? You're one bad event away from zero income. That's terrifying. Does that make sense? So, poor people optimize for stability in poverty. They want a steady paycheck, benefits, predictability, even if it means staying poor. At least it's stable poverty. Rich people optimize for volatility with a safety net. They'll take risks, start businesses, make investments that might fail because even if they fail, they have other income sources. They have assets. They have a safety net. Okay? And this is why when rich people give advice like just start a business or invest in yourself or take risks. Poor people think that's crazy advice because for a poor person, taking a risk means potential homelessness. But for a rich person, taking a risk means maybe this doesn't work out and I try something else. Okay? Completely different games. The poor person is playing not to lose everything. The rich person is playing to multiply everything. Does that make sense? Now, here's where it gets interesting. Let's think about delayed gratification. Okay? Everyone says rich people are good at delayed gratification, right? Rich people can wait for rewards. Poor people want everything now. But think about this more carefully. Who actually delays gratification more? Poor people delay gratification constantly? They skip meals to save money. They wear old clothes for years. They never go on vacation. They never buy anything fun. Their entire life is delayed gratification. Okay. Rich people. Rich people buy what they want when they want it. They go on vacation. They buy nice clothes. They eat at restaurants. So, who's really delaying gratification? Okay. The difference is not willpower. The difference is certainty of return. When a rich person delays gratification, they know the reward is coming. If a rich person invests money, they're confident they'll get returns because they have enough money that even if one investment fails, they have others. Okay? But when a poor person delays gratification, there's no guarantee of reward. If a poor person skips meals to save money, they might save $100 and then their car breaks down and the $100 is gone. So, they sacrificed for nothing. Okay? So, it's not that poor people are bad at delaying gratification. It's that poor people have learned through experience that delaying gratification often leads to no reward. So they're actually being rational when they don't delay gratification. Does that make sense? And this helps us understand something really important. Poor people and rich people are playing two completely different economic games. I call this subtraction versus multiplication. Okay? Poor people play the subtraction game. You start with a little bit of money and every expense subtracts from that money. And the goal is to subtract slowly. Try to make the money last as long as possible. Okay, rich people play the multiplication game. You start with a lot of money and every expense is actually an investment that should multiply your money. And the goal is to multiply as fast as possible. Okay? So, when a poor person's car breaks down, that subtraction minus $500. They're now poorer. When a rich person's car breaks down, they pay $500, but it doesn't affect their wealth at all because their wealth is in assets that are still multiplying. Does that make sense? Or let's think about debt. For poor people, debt is subtraction. It's highinterest credit cards and payday loans. Every month, you're subtracting more and more. You're getting poorer. For rich people, debt is multiplication. It's lowinterest mortgages and business loans. You're using other people's money to buy assets that make you richer. Okay? Same word debt, but completely different meanings, completely different games. And you cannot win a multiplication game using subtraction strategies. Okay. So now let's think about the emergency room decision. If you're rich and you have a minor health issue, like your wrist hurts a little bit, you go to the emergency room immediately. You don't wait. You don't think about the cost. You just go. Why? Because you're playing the multiplication game. Your health is an asset. You invest in maintaining your assets. Okay? But if you're poor and your wrist hurts, you wait. You see if it gets worse. You take some pain medication. You hope it goes away on its own. Why? Because you're playing the subtraction game. Going to the emergency room costs money. Every expense subtracts from your limited resources. So, you wait and you hope you don't have to spend the money. Okay? And we look at this and we say poor people make bad health decisions. But they're not bad decisions. They're rational decisions given the game they're playing. If you're playing subtraction, avoiding expenses is smart. The problem is that sometimes your risk doesn't get better. Sometimes it gets worse. Sometimes waiting turns a minor problem into a major problem. But the poor person was still making the rational choice given their constraints. Does that make sense? Or think about college majors. Rich kid goes to college and their parents say explore, find yourself, try different classes, change your major if you want. This is about discovering your passion. Okay? Why? Because the rich parents are playing the multiplication game. They see college as an investment in their child's network and personal development. The specific degree doesn't matter that much because they know their kid will be fine. They have connections. They have family money. They have a safety net. Okay, poor kid goes to college and everyone tells them, "Pick something practical. Get a degree that guarantees a job. Computer science, engineering, nursing. Don't waste time on art or philosophy or history. You need a job." Okay? Why? Because the poor parents are playing the subtraction game. College costs money. That's a huge subtraction. So, what better result in a highpaying job or the whole thing was a waste? The poor kid can't afford to explore because exploration might lead to no job. And no job means all that money was subtracted for nothing. Does that make sense? So, we have these three fundamental differences. Time horizons, assets versus income, and risk perception. And they all point to the same thing. Poor people and rich people are living in different economic realities. They're playing different games with different rules. And because they're playing different games, advice that works for one group, doesn't work for the other. When a rich person tells a poor person, just invest in index funds, they don't understand that the poor person doesn't have money to invest. When a poor person tells a rich person, you need to budget carefully. The rich person doesn't understand why they would need to budget when their assets generate more than they spend. Okay. So, the question becomes, why does financial advice that works for rich people fail poor people? And the answer is that financial advice assumes you're playing the multiplication game. All financial advice, all the books, all the podcasts, all the courses, they all assume you have surplus money to invest. They assume you can delay gratification and get a reward. They assume you can take risks because you have a safety net. But if you're playing the subtraction game, none of that advice makes sense. Okay. Now, you might be thinking, okay, well, if poor people just learned about wealth, if they understood these concepts, couldn't they escape? And the answer is it's really, really hard. Let me tell you about something I call class defection. Okay, class defection is when a poor person abandons their community and tries to join the rich community. The most common way is marrying into wealth or getting adopted by a rich mentor. Okay? And what happens is you learn by immersion. It's like moving to a foreign country to learn a new language. You're surrounded by people who understand wealth and slowly you start to understand it too. You see how they think. You see how they make decisions. You pick up the cultural codes. Okay, but here's the problem. When you do this, you often lose connection to your origin community. Your family doesn't recognize you anymore. Your old friends think you've changed. You're code switching constantly, acting rich around rich people, acting poor around poor people, and it's exhausting. Okay, I'll give you an example. A poor kid gets a scholarship to an Ivy League school. Suddenly, they're surrounded by rich kids. They learn how rich people talk, how they dress, what they value, how they think about money. The poor kid becomes culturally rich. They learn the language of wealth. But when they go home for Thanksgiving, their family thinks they're acting weird. Why are you talking like that? Why are you so different? The family feels like they lost their kid. And the kid feels like they don't belong anywhere. Not rich enough for rich people, not poor enough for poor people anymore. Okay? And this is why class defection is so rare. It's not just about learning new information. It's about abandoning your entire identity and community. And most people aren't willing to do that. It's too psychologically painful. Does that make sense? So another way of thinking about this is why is it that knowing about money doesn't make you rich? Because wealth isn't about information. It's about circumstance. You can read every book about investing, but if you don't have money to invest, the information is useless. You can understand compound interest perfectly. But if you're living paycheck to paycheck, compound interest doesn't apply to you. Okay. The three things you need to build wealth are money you don't need for survival, time to let investments grow, and psychological safety to take risks. If you don't have all three, you can't build wealth. And poor people by definition don't have these three things. They don't have surplus money. They don't have time because they need returns quickly. They don't have psychological safety because one bad decision means catastrophe. Okay. So when people say why don't poor people just learn about wealth, they're missing the point. Poor people can learn about wealth. They can understand the concepts, but understanding and being able to implement are two different things. It's like explain to someone how to swim when they're drowning. The information is correct, but it's not useful in that moment. Okay, now here's the really interesting part. Why does this system stay stable? Why doesn't it change? And the answer is going to sound crazy, but hear me out. Poor people paradoxically benefit from staying confused about wealth. Okay, let me explain. If a poor person truly understands wealth, truly understands that everything they've been doing is wrong, that they're playing the wrong game, that their entire strategy for life is fundamentally flawed, that's psychologically devastating. Okay? It's much easier to believe, I'm doing the right things. I'm just unlucky. The system is against me, but my decisions are fine. That's a comfortable belief. It protects your ego. It lets you feel like a good person who's just had bad luck. But if you admit that you don't understand wealth, if you admit that you've been playing the wrong game your entire life, that means admitting that you've wasted years, maybe decades, doing the wrong things. And that's too painful. Okay? Plus, there's community reinforcement. Everyone around you thinks the same way. Your family, your friends, your neighbors, they're all playing the subtraction game. They all think having a job is safe. They all think saving cash is smart. They all think rich people just got lucky. So if you suddenly start thinking differently, you're not just rejecting the beliefs, you're rejecting your community. And humans are social creatures. We need community. So most people choose community over truth. Does that make sense? And then there's the admission of waste. If you admit that you don't understand wealth, you're admitting that all those years working hard, all that time spent budgeting, all those sacrifices, they weren't moving you toward wealth. They were just helping you survive poverty. And that's a brutal truth. So, it's psychologically easier to stay confused, to keep believing that you're doing fine. You just need a little more luck. Okay? So, the system stays stable because both groups benefit from the confusion. Rich people benefit because if poor people understood wealth, they'd demand systemic change. They'd say, "Wait, the game is rigged. This isn't fair." But as long as poor people think they're playing the same game as rich people, just losing, they blame themselves instead of the system. Okay? And poor people benefit from staying confused because understanding the truth is too painful. So they maintain their beliefs. They stay in their communities. They keep playing the subtraction game. And they pass these beliefs on to their children. And the cycle continues generation after generation. The rich stay rich because they teach their kids the multiplication game. The poor stay poor because they teach their kids the subtraction game. Okay. So when we ask why do some people build generational wealth while others stay broke forever? The answer isn't about intelligence or work ethic or financial literacy. It's about which game you're playing. And you can't choose which game to play. The game is chosen for you by your birth circumstances. If you're born into a family playing multiplication, you learn multiplication. If you're born into a family playing subtraction, you learn subtraction. and switching games. That requires abandoning everything you know, everyone you love, and admitting that everything you believed was wrong. Very few people can do that. Okay. What do poor people get wrong about how wealth actually works? Everything. Not because they're stupid, but because they're playing a different game where the rules are different. And the tragic thing is that the rules of the subtraction game make perfect sense if you're trying to survive. They just don't build wealth. And nobody tells you that you're playing the wrong game. Everyone just tells you to play harder, play smarter, play better. But you can't win the multiplication game using subtraction strategies. It's impossible. Does that make sense, guys? Okay.

full_text: Welcome to Predictive History Jang. Today we explore why Iran is considered an unconquerable fortress of natural geography and strategic brilliance. For centuries, many powerful empires have looked at this vast territory and quickly realized that invading it would be an absolute nightmare. The true power of this nation does not just lie in its military forces or political alliances, but in its physical terrain. Iran is completely surrounded by massive natural barriers that serve as an impenetrable shield against any massive ground invasion from foreign armies. Looking closely at a topographic map, you will immediately notice the towering mountain ranges that form a giant wall around the country. In the southwest, the mighty Zagras mountains stretch for hundreds of miles, creating a formidable obstacle for any advancing military ground force. These jagged peaks and deep valleys make it virtually impossible for heavy armored vehicles and large infantry divisions to cross with ease. Any invading army trying to navigate these treacherous mountain passes would immediately find themselves trapped in narrow corridors without any escape routes. Meanwhile, the northern border is heavily protected by the Albor's mountains which stand tall like a group of silent ancient stone guardians. These towering northern peaks effectively block invasions from the Caspian Sea region, forcing enemies to rethink their entire military strategy and approach. Even if an enemy miraculously breaches the outer mountain walls, they will face an even deadlier challenge waiting in the central region. The interior is dominated by two massive and extremely unforgiving deserts known widely as the Great Salt Desert and the Lut Desert. These vast wastelands hold records for some of the highest temperatures ever recorded on the surface of our entire planet throughout history. Searing heat and absolute lack of water mean that no modern army can successfully sustain a long military campaign across these sands. Advancing troops would quickly suffer from severe dehydration, equipment failures, and massive logistical nightmares before even reaching any major populated urban centers. Because the deserts are essentially impassible, invaders are forcefully funneled into a few narrow transit corridors located between the rough mountain ranges. This predictable movement allows the defending military forces to carefully prepare devastating ambushes and easily destroy enemy convoys trapped in these bottlenecks. Moving away from the harsh inland deserts, we must also examine the incredibly strategic and highly fortified southern coastline of the nation. Iran possesses over 2,800 km of rugged coastline that directly overlooks the globally important waters of the Persian Gulf. Unlike flat sandy beaches, much of this coastline features steep rocky cliffs and mountainous terrain that stretches right down to the water. This unique coastal geography provides the perfect natural camouflage for hiding countless underground missile silos and advanced anti-hship military defense systems. Enemy naval fleets approaching these shores would face a constant barrage of hidden missiles launched from virtually undetectable positions inside the mountains. The most critical geographic asset of all is undoubtedly the famous straight of Hormas located at the very entrance of the Gulf. This narrow waterway is the most important oil transit choke point in the entire world. Handling roughly 20% of global petroleum supplies. By controlling this vital maritime passage, the nation holds a massive economic weapon that can easily disrupt the entire global financial system. Any direct military conflict would immediately threaten the safe passage of these oil tankers, causing global energy prices to skyrocket almost instantly. This immense economic leverage forces foreign superpowers to think twice before launching any aggressive military campaigns against this heavily fortified sovereign nation. But geography alone does not win wars. The human element and national spirit play a massive role in defending this ancient land. To truly understand their defensive mindset, we must look back at the brutal and devastating war fought against Iraq in the 80s. Despite facing severe internal political turmoil and significant ethnic divisions at the time, the entire population quickly united against the foreign invaders. The shared trauma and ultimate survival during that long conflict forged an unbreakable sense of national resilience that still exists today everywhere. Whenever an external power threatens their sovereignty, the people historically put aside their domestic differences to fiercely protect their beloved home country. This fierce patriotism acts as an invisible but incredibly strong shield, making the prospect of a foreign occupation completely impossible to achieve. Furthermore, the sheer physical size of the country provides an overwhelming advantage known in military science as the concept of strategic depth. Covering over 1.6 million square kilm, the nation is larger than France, Germany, Spain, and the United Kingdom combined together. Occupying such a gigantic land mass would require millions of foreign troops and an astronomical financial budget that no single country can afford. The vast distances between major cities mean that supply lines would be stretched dangerously thin, leaving them highly vulnerable to guerilla attacks. Invaders would find themselves constantly fighting a war of attrition, slowly bleeding resources and manpower in a hostile and unforgiving geographic environment. The local military doctrine is specifically designed to take full advantage of this massive space, utilizing decentralized command structures and asymmetric warfare. They do not need to win a conventional battle. They simply need to outlast the enemy and make the occupation incredibly painful. Every mountain pass, every desert highway, and every coastal cave is meticulously mapped and integrated into a comprehensive national defense strategy network. Underground military bases are carved deep inside solid rock, completely safe from even the most advanced bunker busting bombs available today worldwide. These subterranean fortresses house ballistic missiles, combat drones, and vital command centers, ensuring that the military can always strike back with power. The integration of modern military technology with ancient natural defenses creates a hybrid warfare environment that is exceptionally difficult to counter successfully. Enemy satellites might monitor the surface, but they can never truly see what lies hidden beneath the thick layers of mountain stone. Let us take another look at the Zagris Mountains, where local fighters have trained for decades to perfect the art of ambushes. Small mobile units armed with anti-tank weapons can easily destroy entire armored columns while remaining completely hidden among the rocky terrain. The harsh winters in these elevated regions bring heavy snowfall, further complicating any military logistics and severely slowing down enemy troop movements. Moving towards the east, the terrain remains equally hostile, sharing rugged borders with neighboring countries that are notoriously difficult to properly police. This eastern frontier acts as another significant buffer zone, absorbing potential threats before they can ever reach the important central political hubs. The capital city of Tehran itself is strategically nestled right at the foothills of the towering Albor's mountains for maximum physical protection. To capture the capital, an invading force would have to fight through hundreds of miles of heavily defended urban and mountainous zones. It is a logistical puzzle that has frustrated brilliant military planners and powerful generals from various global superpowers for many long decades. In the southern waters, the Navy relies on a massive fleet of small, highly maneuverable speedboats armed with deadly torpedoes and missiles. These fast attack craft employ swarm tactics overwhelming large enemy warships by striking from multiple directions simultaneously before quickly retreating to safety. The shallow and confined waters of the Persian Gulf heavily restrict the movement of massive aircraft carriers, making them highly vulnerable targets. Coastal defense cruise missiles are constantly locked onto the shipping lanes, ready to be fired at a moment's notice if hostilities begin. This overlapping web of coastal defenses ensures that any hostile fleet entering the Gulf takes on an unacceptable level of deadly risk. Beyond the physical weapons, the country has also developed a highly sophisticated asymmetric warfare network extending far beyond its own national borders. By supporting various regional allied groups, they have successfully pushed their defensive lines outward, creating a complex web of strategic proxy deterrence. This forward defense strategy means that any attack on their homeland could trigger a massive coordinated response across the entire Middle East. The combination of impenetrable geography, vast strategic depth, and a deeply unified population makes this nation a truly unique fortress of power. No computer simulation or advanced military war game has ever produced a scenario where a foreign power successfully conquers and pacifies Iran. The immense cost in human lives and economic resources would simply be too catastrophic for any modern democracy to justify to voters. The Great Salt Desert alone has swallowed entire ancient armies, and it stands ready to do the exact same to modern forces. Even Alexander the Great faced immense difficulties navigating these treacherous lands, losing many of his finest soldiers to the unforgiving natural elements. Today, the technology has evolved significantly, but the fundamental geographic reality of this vast mountainous plateau remains completely unchanged and incredibly hostile. The mountains still block the tanks, the deserts still drain the water supplies, and the narrow valleys still create the perfect ambushes. Let us dive deeper into the psychological aspect of this defense where historical memory fuels a powerful narrative of constant national resistance. From school children to elderly citizens, the population is constantly reminded of their sacred duty to protect their borders from foreign interference. This cultural conditioning ensures that a potential occupier would face constant civil disobedience, sabotage, and armed resistance from the local civilian population. Every village nestled in the mountains could quickly transform into a heavily armed rebel stronghold, making pacification an absolutely impossible military objective. The sheer diversity of the terrain means that an invading army must carry different equipment for snow, desert, and urban combat simultaneously. Supplying an army with winter gear for the mountains and cooling systems for the deserts creates an unsolvable nightmare for logistics officers. Road networks are deliberately designed to cross vulnerable bridges and narrow tunnels that can be easily destroyed to halt any enemy advance. Once these vital infrastructure points are blown up, invading forces would be completely stranded in hostile territory without any hope of resupply. The air force might dominate the skies, but wars are ultimately won and lost by the soldiers standing firmly on the ground. And on the ground, the natural environment of this region is the ultimate undisputed champion, defeating human ambition time and time again. In the modern era, the focus has shifted towards cyber warfare and drones, but geography remains the ultimate foundation of national security. Electronic jammers and anti-aircraft batteries are perfectly hidden within deep mountain valleys, creating blind spots that completely confuse enemy targeting systems. The nation has effectively transformed its own geological features into a massive living weapon that constantly works against any hostile foreign intruder. It is a brilliant masterclass in utilizing what nature provides to offset the massive technological advantages held by wealthier global military superpowers. As global tensions continue to rise, understanding these fundamental geographic realities is absolutely crucial for predicting the future of international geopolitical conflicts. Any politician or general who ignores the mighty Zagris mountains or the scorching le desert does so at their own terrible peril. The unconquerable fortress of the Middle East will continue to stand strong, protected by its ancient stones, burning sands, and resilient people. We have thoroughly explored the deep valleys, the harsh deserts, the strategic coastlines, and the unbreakable spirit that defines this powerful nation. The lessons of history clearly show that nature is often the greatest military commander, dictating the ultimate outcome of human geopolitical struggles. The geographic shield of this ancient land remains as formidable today as it was during the times of the great Persian empires. Thank you for taking this fascinating journey with us to uncover the profound secrets behind one of the most secure nations worldwide. This has been Predictive History Jang. Please remember to like and subscribe for more deep dives into history, geography, and global strategy.

full_text: Why is a soft-spoken educator from Beijing suddenly being hailed by some as a modern-day Nostradamus? In early 20200, a single dense academic lecture by Professor Jang Shu Chin ignited the internet, cutting through the noise of traditional political commentary. While most analysts were caught off guard by shifting global alliances and sudden conflicts, Jen seemed to have expected them. His fame isn't built on mysticism or crystal balls, but on a methodology so cold and calculated that it borders on the clinical. In this exploration, we dive into the mind of the architect behind predictive history to understand how he decodes the chaotic atmosphere of our modern world. Jen's framework, which he calls predictive history, is far removed from the emotional rhetoric of cable news. It is an intellectual structure built on three unbreakable pillars. First, he identifies historical patterns, arguing that human power structures repeat themselves across centuries. Second, he analyzes geopolitical incentives, viewing nations not as heroes or villains, but as logical actors driven by geography and survival. Finally, he applies game theory, treating international relations as a massive multiplayer strategic game. By combining these, Jang attempts to do for history what Isaac Azimov's psycho history did for fiction. create a mathematical certainty out of human behavior. To understand the first pillar, we have to look back to ancient Greece. Jen draws heavily from Thusidities, the father of scientific history. He famously compared the recent overextension of global superpowers to the Athenian Sicilian expedition, a moment where a dominant empire, blinded by its own strength, committed to a distant war that it could not logistically sustain. By mapping these ancient blueprints onto the 21st century, Jenang argues that we aren't living through unique events. Instead, we are seeing the latest iteration of a cycle that has played out dozens of times since the dawn of civilization. Nowhere is this analysis more striking than in his case study of the Middle East, specifically the tensions surrounding Iran. While others focus on rhetoric, Jang looks at the map. He points to the jagged mountain ranges, the narrow straight of Hormuz, and the sprawling desert basins that define the region's tactical reality. In his view, these geographic features act as a natural fortress that negates conventional military superiority. By understanding the physical board on which this game is played, Jenang predicts that any direct conflict will inevitably devolve into a stalemate that favors the defender's endurance over the attacker's power. This leads to Jang's most controversial theory, the war of attrition. He argues that in the modern era, military might is secondary to logistical resilience. A superpower, he suggests, is like a massive engine that requires a constant flow of resources to function. If a strategic competitor can successfully disrupt that flow by targeting energy pipelines or destabilizing key logistical hubs like the Gulf States, they don't need to win a single battle. They only need to wait for the engine to stall. This focus on the lynchpins of global energy infrastructure is why his forecasts regarding the Persian Gulf have been so hauntingly accurate. The accuracy of these predictions has naturally invited heavy skepticism. Critics argue that Jang may be a victim of selection bias, highlighting his successful guesses while ignoring the complexities he misses. They ask, "Can history really be boiled down to a game of chess?" Jiang himself acknowledges the debate, maintaining that his goal isn't to be a prophet, but to provide a logical alternative to the chaos narrative. He moves the needle away from what will happen next and toward a deeper inquiry. Why is this the most probable outcome based on the incentives in play? It is analysis as a form of architecture. As we look toward an increasingly fractured global landscape, the work of Professor Xanguin offers a rare grounded perspective. Whether you agree with his conclusions or find his methods too deterministic, his shift toward a datadriven historical framework is undeniable. He has reminded us that beneath the headlines of the day, there are deep structural currents moving the world. In the end, predictive history isn't just about knowing the future. It's about having the tools to navigate the present with a clear head and a steady hand. The game continues, but now we have a map.

full_text: Good day. This is Demetri Lceris coming to you for Reason to Resist from Montreal, Canada on January 22nd, 2026. Today I'm very pleased to be joined by Professor Ciang. Professor Ciang is a Chinese Canadian educator, writer, historian, and geopolitical theorist based in Beijing, China. He's also the founder and host of a widely followed and important YouTube channel called Predictive History. Thanks very much for joining us for the first time, Professor Jen. >> Thanks so much, Demet. I'm a huge fan of your work. >> Uh, thank you so much. Um, first, uh, you know, I wanted to discuss with you initially when I reached out to you uh, Mark Carney's, uh, just completed trip to Beijing and also his widely discussed uh, speech at Davos. But, uh, in light of events over the last couple of days, I've added to the agenda uh, the looming attack or what appears to be a looming attack on Iran. uh and uh I'm going to start with that subject. Before I do, I just like to remind our audience to like and share this video if they find it informative and uh to uh please subscribe to our YouTube channel if uh you haven't already done so. So with that, uh professor, I want to show you an excerpt from an interview this week of retired US General Jack Keane. In this clip from uh a Fox News interview, Keen is asked whether he believes uh that an attack on Iran by the Trump regime is imminent. And this is what he had to say. >> Sure. >> Oh. Oh, okay. >> And then there's no doubt about it. I mean, we're bringing these resources to bear, and it's likely because we had to expand the target set. And why are we expanding it? Because we have to consider what is Iran going to do in retaliation? Our bases are vulnerable. What are they going to do about Israel? They're going to attack Israel likely. >> And they're vulnerable. And what are we going to do about their ballistic missiles? Are we going to take them down before they fire them at our bases and also at Israel? Likely. Are we going to kill leaders? Certainly. Our IRGC members, the people that are oppressing the people, absolutely. So, I think the target set is expanded. resources are being brought in to deal with an expanded target set, which is what we do. >> That's right, folks. That's what we do. Uh now last week, Professor Jiang, uh you gave an interview, I believe it was on, uh Danny Hiong uh in which you talked about the possibility of an attack on Iran. And um you uh expressed the view as I understand it that uh the US government would have considerable difficulty toppling the current government of Iran and um that would more likely to turn instead to uh easy winds in places like Greenland, Canada, and Cuba. Um, I'm curious to know whether in light of what has transpired over the last two to three days and in light also of uh retired General Keane's commentary, has your assessment changed? Do you think that an attack on Iran is not likely at this stage or do you think one is virtually inevitable? >> Yeah. So, let me clarify my comments. So, so my feeling is that they will attack Iran. Uh, they will use air strikes. It will be very impressive. They will hit a lot of military targets. be very sustained, but air strikes by themselves are not effective. And eventually Trump's going to halt air strikes and move his attention elsewhere, but then he'll resume air strikes at a later point. So I think that um this US Iran conflict will be uh in the background throughout 2026. Um, unless Trump is willing to commit ground troops, he cannot actually achieve any of his war aims in uh Iran. The regime is far too resilient, far too resourceful, uh far too cohesive. So that's what I meant. I I I do think that Trump will strike uh Iran because that's always been part of the plan. Remember back in his first term, there's a clear trend towards confrontation with Iran. So Trump immediately withdrew from the nuclear deal. Uh then he um transferred the US embassy from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem which is extremely provocative. Uh he initiated something called the Abraham Accords uh which sought to broad peace between Israel and Saudi Arabia. And the most provocative thing he did of course was in June 2020 he authorized the assassination of General Salammani who was the Iranian de facto envoy in the Middle East. And that essentially is a declaration of war. So if he had sent him office then we would have seen uh mil military strikes against Iran. He lost in 2020 but now he's back in the second term. Uh he is going to continue his the policies uh from his first term which is to seek regime change in Iran. Mhm. Uh I would add to that list, by the way, and I'm sure you're aware of this, uh the uh recognition by the Trump regime in the first administration of uh Israel's theft of the Syrian Golan Heights. Uh we could probably go on and on, but uh I think people get the picture. Now, Iran has long warned that if pushed, it's willing uh and able to close the Strait of Hormuz. If it did that for a significant period of time, let's say 6 months or even more, what would you expect the impact on the global economy to be? So if Iran were to close all the surf moves, it would really be the nuclear option. It'd be worse than a nuclear bomb because it would paralyze the global economy. So most of Southeast Asia, specifically China, uh Japan and South Korea depend on most of its oil and energy from the Middle East. Um, and so if that were to happen, then these economies were to shutter and they would have to respond militarily, especially China and Japan. Um, and so that that really is the trump card that Iran can play. So I imagine that we still have pretty far to go on the escalation ladder before Iran um plays that trump card. So, I imagine what's going to happen um and it's just my best guess as to what's going to happen is that Trump's going to authorize um air strikes and then uh is uh Iran is going to retaliate against Israel because Israel doesn't participate in the air strikes. It's also going to Iran's is also going to retaliate against uh local um Middle East allies including Jordan. Um and uh Iran will strike at uh US air uh US bases in the region for its proxies. um Hezbollah and the Hufi specifically. So um I believe there's an escalation ladder and we have to climb pretty far up the escalation ladder before Iran closes a few moves because if they would do that this would basically be World War II. Uh it would draw in a lot of nations and um the consequences could is it's not really contained anymore. Uh it would be very unpredictable and unprecedented if if Iran were a close officer of Humus. Now, uh, a couple of days ago, uh, a publication called Defense Security Asia, uh, published an article stating that there were unconfirmed reports, that's the way they described them, of a rapid Chinese military airlift into Iran involving up to 16 cargo aircraft. That would be quite a haul potentially, of military equipment. What do you make of these reports, Professor Jiang? >> Right. So from the Chinese perspective, um what Trump is really doing is initiating a long-term strategy of strangling China economically. If you read the national security strategy which came out last month, it's very explicit that um um the United States cannot challenge uh China in the southeast uh Asia militarily. So what what it's going to do is that it's going to use its proxies like Japan and it's going to strangle uh China economically. Now the problem the problem with China is that um if it's its major strength is its exports. So if the United States were to close off the first island chain, right, which is Japan, Taiwan, the Philippines, um, and if there were a war in Iran, then China would be strangled economically because it could not access uh, oil. So I know that Russia is providing 20% of um, China's energy needs, but 20% is not a lot. And the reality is that Russia is having problems meeting that demand right now. So, it's going to take, you know, 5 to 10 years before the Trans Siberian pipeline is um online and Russ and Russia can provide more energy to China. And that's why the United States is going to strike now from a Chinese perspective. I'm saying this this from a Chinese military perspective that the real reason that Trump is going after Iran is to destroy Chinese economy. So, here China have no choice but to bolster Iran. And um so they're they're going to send weapon systems, they're going to send financing, they're going to do whatever they can uh to support Iran. Um but but but there are some red lines. So China will not send soldiers and China will not uh declare war on the United States. >> Do you think that China might send naval vessels into the region? >> Yeah. So the issue is that um China doesn't have a blue water navy to challenge um the American Navy, the blue water navy. But I think what's going to happen this year is that very quickly Russia is going to transform its shuttle fleet. It's it's about a thousand ships into a blue water navy. There's no way that Russia can defeat America in the oceans. Uh but Russia at least has to challenge America. It basically has to um basically um create a war of attrition in the seas. that would hamper uh America's aggression against Iran and China would provide financing for Russia's bluewater navy. So I think that's the what's most likely to happen. >> So let's turn now to uh a less uh violent topic, namely the subject of Mark Carney's much discussed speech at Davos. Uh the most widely discussed aspect of this speech, I think, is Carney's acknowledgement that the rules-based order is, as he put it, a partial fiction. Uh now, he didn't uh that implies that some part of it was true, and he didn't tell us which part, if any, of the rules-based order was true or based on reality. But in any case, um he went on to explain what he believed should replace the rules-based order. and he referred to this new foreign policy paradigm as values-based realism which he said actually came from Finland's president Alexander Stub and in the following excerpt from Carney's Davos speech Carney explains what he means by values-based realism what does it mean for middle powers to live the truth first it means naming reality stop invoking rules-based international order as though it still functions as advertised call Call it what it is, a system of intensifying great power rivalry where the most powerful pursue their interests using economic integration as coercion. It means acting consistently, applying the same standards to allies and rivals. When middle powers criticize economic intimidation from one direction but stay silent when it comes from another, we are keeping the sign in the window. It means building what we claim to believe in rather than waiting for the old order to be restored. It means creating institutions and agreements that function as described. And it means reducing the leverage that enables coercion. >> Now, Professor, we just heard Mark Carney say that values-based realism means applying the same standards to allies and rivals. Uh, and I must confess that I'm deeply skeptical that Canada will now apply the same standards to the West's allies and rivals. And I'm going to give but one example, and that is within 48 hours of Carney delivering this speech, Israel murdered 11 Palestinians in the Gaza Strip, including three more journalists and two more children. Uh, as you can see from this uh, report uh, by Thompson Reuters, I've seen no statement from any Canadian government official condemning or even expressing concern about these atrocities. Uh, they have been virtually silent the entire time that Israel has killed over 100 children in what is supposed to be a ceasefire. Uh moreover, after more than two years of genocide, Mark Carney's government has imposed no sanctions on Netanyahu or Galand, both of whom have been indicted by the ICC. It has imposed no sanctions on major Israeli corporations, not even those in the uh military-industrial sector. It's imposed no sanctions on Israel's military leaders, but it continues to maintain sweeping sanctions on Russia and Iran, uh both of which it regards as geopolitical rivals of the West. Uh so again to be perfectly blunt about a professor I think that Mark Carney's speech was essentially a marketing exercise. That's my suspicion and I hope I'm wrong. Uh he knows that the whole world now sees the reality of the rules-based international order. Uh that it is a fraud and that the concept has been discredited and therefore I suspect and I could be wrong again that western leaders want to rebrand uh their foreign policy as a values-based realism. But there doesn't appear to be any meaningful distinction between that and the now completely discredited rules-based international order. So that's basically my skeptical view. Uh but I'm very curious to know what is your take not just on this particular question more broadly on Mark Carney's speech at Davos. >> Yeah. So um overall I agree your sentiment. Um I I think you tend to be very right about these sort of things. It's better to be skeptical than it is to be um naive. Right. So um I I did read Mark Carney's speech and he makes in his speech two major historical illusions. The first is to facilities and facilities is writing about the pel pelpian war and the war was fought between Athens and Sparta. So um Athens um had uh defeated the Persians and because of that the um uh the uh Aianc Greeks were very thankful to the Athenians just as the Europeans were very thankful to the Americans for defeating fascism in Europe. And so um after the Persian wars they created something called the Delian League. It was meant to be a mutual defense pack. But over time Athens as a hedgeimon started to exploit its allies. it it became a mafia regime and it started to extort its allies. So, and then its allies had no choice but to seek relief from Sparta. So, I think that with this Fidili's illusion, Mark Carney is is sort of like hinting to his audience that we are at this moment when uh America's former allies have turned into their va vassels. And so, America is going to exploit us. America is going to steal our wealth. America is going to fight make us fight wars we cannot possibly win. um probably in Ukraine and so we need to band together and seek relief from another Sparta. Today Sparta would be China, right? So I think that's the first historical illusion he's making. The second historical illusion he's making of course is to Valhava's famous uh essay the p the power of the powerless. Um and and you know it's very ironic he does that because uh in his speech he says that okay uh let's be honest from day one we knew this rulesbased national order was a fraud it was hypocritical but we benefit from it okay because we were friends of the hegeimon the um great empire and so we benefited but now that the empire is turning against us Now that the empire wants to cannibalize us, we must now speak truth to power. Okay. So you can imagine the hypocrisy. Um so I don't think it's it's much of a strategy. Okay. Because I think like anyone with a brain, you know, which obviously precludes Canadians, but anyone with a brain can see through the hypocrisy, the travesty um and the self-indulgence in his speech. Um it's not really a grand strategy. It's more like it's more of a lamont, right? It's it's lamentation of, you know, like we used to be best friends America. We benefited a great deal. Now America has abandoned us. We're a jud lover and now we're forced to uh band together and seek a new ally in China. So I read the speech more like that. Um you know and he has like three grand suggestions for moving forward. The first of course is to speak truth to power to recognize the severity of the situation and be honest with ourselves. The second is to create as you say um uh a new system uh of rules-based international order of institutions that could counterbalance the bullying of America. And the third is to uh rely more heavily on Canadian capital and talent. The problem Canada you know this is that everyone's fleeing right capital fleeing talent is fleeing Canada. So Canada's being hollowed out. So um you know as a banker and you know Mark Carney used to work for Goldman Sachs uh he used to be governor of the bank of Canada and then he went to uh England became the first foreign governor of the bank of England. He is a banker who serves transnational capital and the reality is that Canada uh the nation I hate to say this because I grew up there right um but Canada it's a toxic asset right now. housing prices are are like, you know, skyhigh. Um the Canadian economy, 40% of the Canadian economy um is real estate speculation. Uh Canada is this glorified money laundering operation. Um it's lost all its legitimacy by by supporting this genocide in Gaza. Um there are hundreds of thousands of homeless in Canada. Yet Carney still continue to send tens of billions of dollars to Ukraine to fight a pointless war causing you know about a million Ukrainians to die for no particular reason. Um these um Canadian companies refuse to invest in the country and many of them are fleeing the country. So Canada is a toxic asset and what you do with toxic asset as a banker is you sell it to foreigners. And who are the foreigners? The Qataris and the Chinese. Okay. So that's the grand strategy that Carney has and everything else like this grand rhetoric about a rules based national order. It's just as you say marketing. That's all it is. Okay. Don't pay any attention to it. Watch what he does. So what he's going to do is this. He's going to call in an early election probably late spring. Okay. April. He's going to win by his landslide. He's have a super majority and then he'll start to asset strip Canada. Okay? That's the plan. Pretty simple. >> That's quite distressing for those of us who live here. But I I find that an entirely uh persuasive uh prediction about what is likely to happen in the months ahead. Uh now let's let's imagine uh Professor Jiang that you uh were uh asked to provide advice to the Canadian government about how to deal with the current predicament. You know, I know this is a huge and complex question, but I am curious to know uh how you would uh deal with it. And let me be clear. I've been complaining for years that our governments, successive liberal and conservative governments have done so much to increase our dependency upon the United States and to de-industrialize this company, to deprive this country, uh to deprive us of the capacity to produce domestically the things that are necessary for the well-being of the Canadian population. Ground forces, infantry, and mechanized units at the bottom. Why? Because they're the most cost effective for sustained operations. What's above them? Armor and artillery, tanks, mobile firepower. They're more expensive to field and maintain. Then you have naval assets. Then you have air power. Does it make sense? This is the force structure pyramid. And the force pyramid represents resource efficiency. How much does it cost to sustain each type of capability? It costs relatively less to deploy infantry, but it costs vastly more to maintain continuous air operations. This is critical because wars of attrition are fundamentally about who can sustain operations longer. Does it make sense, guys? The problem is that the United States has developed an inverted pyramid. Meaning that air power is dominant, then naval power, then armor, then ground forces treated as a last resort. You cannot win a sustained war with this structure. The moment you try to fight a prolonged conflict with this model, you face insurmountable challenges because you cannot sustain the operational tempo. Now, I know this is counterintuitive, but the Gulf War in 1991, the Iraq invasion in 2003, these were not wars of attrition. Okay? because the Americans had such overwhelming advantage and Iraq was so militarily degraded that these were essentially one-sided military operations. Right? Because your most sustainable capability is ground forces and your least sustainable is continuous air operations. You cannot afford to rely primarily on air power in a prolonged conflict. Iran wants to achieve regional dominance and force US withdrawal. Israel wants to destroy Iran's military capability, but also benefits from a prolonged US commitment to the region. And Saudi Arabia wants Iran weakened but also wants to reduce overall US influence. So by understanding how each actor perceives the game, we can predict behavior. The United States wants to neutralize Iran quickly. But Israel has incentives for this to become prolonged because a quick resolution means America can disengage. Saudi Arabia wants Iran weakened but not permanent US dominance. And Iran wants to impose costs until America gives up. And how do you make America give up? full_text: Today's guest is Professor Jang. He's a prominent educator and writer. He was born in China in the 70s, but later immigrated to Toronto, Canada, where he grew up. He graduated with distinction in English literature from Yale College, but eventually returned to China where he taught at some of the most prestigious educational institutions where he advocated for reform in greater creativity. His later work expanded into geopolitical commentary and predictive history. His work was widely respected in the academic and journalistic world, but recently Jen also exploded in popularity on social media when one of his videos predicting the Iran war went viral on YouTube. In less than a month, Jen's YouTube channel went from obscurity to half a million subscribers with viewers pouring through his catalog of videos making all sorts of geopolitical predictions such as the Trump re-election, its implications on the Russia Ukraine war, and the event. With his newfound popularity, Professor Jen hopes to expand the work of his YouTube channel, Predictive History, where he hopes to help humanity gain more control over the present by understanding its past and estimating the future. So, it is my pleasure and honor to introduce to you all today, Professor Jang, so we can discuss what the wars in the Middle East, Eastern Europe, and potentially East Asia tell us about the future of human civilization. Professor, welcome to the channel. >> Thank you so much for inviting me. So, professor, if you could just please explain to my audience what predictive history is and why you believe this field of study is so important to humanity. >> Yeah, sure. So, um if you read history books, you will um see there are many divergent views out there. Um there's like 10,000 ways of understanding anything. Um and so the idea of predictive history is let's try to systemize this. Let's try to create a system in which we can know if we're right or wrong about the way we understand history. And so my argument is that if history is done correctly, um if the framework for understanding of history is correct, then we should be able to do three things. The first is we should be able to connect the past, which is to say we should be able to turn uh the history into a coherent narrative about how we came into being. Second um is we should be able to explain the present. Uh why is the world the way it is today? The third thing is to be able to predict future events to break predict history. So if the framework for history is able to accomplish all three then we know that we're approaching the truth. Now the idea is that in every conception of history there are values and assumptions embedded in that ethical model. And if we understand the values and assumptions, we can then make a prediction model based on these values and assumptions. So predictive history, it's a way for us to better understand ourselves so that we can have better control over our own lives and it's a way to um basically push history forward so that historians come to an agreement on major historical events. So that's the general gist. So what is it that you understood about the past and the present moment that helped you predict correctly the 12-day Iran Israel war? >> Right. So what clued me in that the conflict would arise between uh the United States and Iran is that in January 20 uh 20 um Donald Trump when he in in his first term he ordered an assassination of General Solommani. um the Iranian de facto ambassador in the Middle East and um that was an extremely important geopolitical event and I don't think that people really understood how important it was because historically in that region if you kill an ambassador that's a direct provocation of war. The um the the other side is now forced to go to war. So the classic example is when Persia invaded uh Greece the mainland Greece in 490 BC um ficidities sorry no not not fid but Herod Heroditus writes about this in his book the histories well the reason why Persia launched this massive invasion of the Greek mainland was because Athens and Sparta killed their ambassadors they literally threw their ambassadors into the well and when you do that in that world then that's a declaration of war so the United States basically by killing General Solommani declared war on Iran. And at that point, it was just a question of timeline. When would this happen? And this got derailed because um Trump lost to Biden in the um general election. But but but if he if he were come back to office, then you could um assume that the timeline would resume and that eventually uh the conflict would arise. So that sort of clue me in um that a war would arise. And then what what I did next was okay, who are the actors pushing for war and why would a war happen? And so as I did more research, I discovered things like Christian Zionism, the Apac um lobby in the United States. Saudi Arabia has a vested interest in making sure that Iran um is um that that there's a regime change in Iran because of the Shia Sunni conflict. So there are lots of reasons, there's lots of um actors who want to see a US Iran war. And if that's the case, then you can you can um rest assure that a war will eventually erupt between um United States and Iraq. >> Were you surprised at all with the timeline? I think uh a lot of people understood obviously after October 7th that this is a eventuality or a potential uh event that's going to take place, especially with the collapse of Syria, Bashar al-Assad. So were you surprised at all that it happened, you know, in June of this year or were you expecting it maybe to take a little longer? Um to be honest with you, I expect I expected the timeline to be much more extended because you know for what to happen you need to prepare um lots of things. You you have the proper military organization. You have you need to have buy in from the military from the Pentagon. You need buyin from different political factions. You need to like lay the groundwork. And um I'm just surprised by how how um abrupt it has been. Um, it's happened far quickly quick quick quicker than I expected. But not only that, but the timeline seems to be much more condensed. Um, I was expecting maybe like a two-year runup, but it's entirely possible that by next year before the midterms, uh, you'll have US ground troops in Iran. So, I'm I'm so I'm so taken back by how fast things are moving uh along. I watched a video of yours recently and you talked about how Iran's objective in order for it to have the advantage over the United States is to bait them into a ground war. Obviously, after the Iraq war, that's like the dumbest mistake the United States could make. But you argue that it could still happen. How and why is that the case? If that is like, it's not that, you know, I can't imagine the US doing something against its own interests. Of course, it can. But something as catastrophic as putting troops on the ground in Iran, that is uh very surprising to hear. So how and why do you see that being a possibility? >> Yeah. So um I use game theory analysis in order to make predictions. So I'm looking at the different actors in the region. Israel, Saudi Arabia, the United States, Iran are the four, uh, ma major actors, and I'm trying to figure out how they benefit as this game progresses. So, um, if you're United States, then you definitely want to cement control over the Middle East for both trade purposes and for resources, right? Because, um, Iran is basically the center of the world. If you control Iran, you control all global trade. you negate uh bricks uh you negate belt and road. Okay, you basically um isolate Russia and and China. So from a geop strategic geopolitical perspective, United States definitely wants control of Iran. But if you're so if you're Israel and you're Saudi Arabia, you don't want to become a vassel to the American Empire. You don't want to become a proxy. uh you don't want to lose your sovereignty especially Israel because um the entire point of the nation of Israel is to assert Jewish sovereignty uh which they believe to be the biblical right. So in order for that to happen the United States needs to be destroyed. The United States needs to lose a ground war against Iran. So Saudi Arabia and Israel are both heavily invested in seeing a US ground invasion. And um if you watch my previous videos um you will know that Saudi Arabia and Israel are politically very strong in Washington DC. They spend a lot of money. They spend a lot of resources. They've co-opted a lot of politicians. Um Donald Trump has direct line to both Saudi Arabia and Israel through his son-in-law Jared Kushner. And Donald Trump has always maintained strong relations with Saudi Arabia. like what was his where was his first foreign trip overseas in a second term Saudi Arabia right so um Saudi Arabia has a much more influence in uh the Trump white house than than we imagine so both Israel and Saudi Arabia want to see a ground war Iran want wants to see a ground war as well and there are certain steps that Iran can take to solely provoke a ground war if Iran at any point would close off the short humus then that will compel a ground invasion in order to reopen that of Hormus. Obviously, if Iran were to close the street of Hormus, that would collapse the entire global economy, right? Southeast Asia depends depends uh on the Middle East for its oil. The Middle East needs to export its oil to be economically viable. Um so, Iran has a series of uh maneuvers, a series of steps to compel a ground invasion. The last thing I will say is the United States uh military has become an imperial bureaucracy. It doesn't really have strategic foresight. It doesn't really do strategic planning anymore. It's very reactive. It's very intent on maintaining its hijgemony. It doesn't really look forward. And at the end of the day, um let's just say the United States loses a ground war against Iran. What are the consequences? Well, the consequence the major consequences is you have these very unpleasant congressional ser testimonies in Washington DC where the US military is haranged by congressmen. That's about it. I mean it it's not a big deal if the United States loses a uh ground war in Iran. But you have these very powerful actors that want to compel a ground war. And that's why using game theory, I think a ground war is more likely than it than it is not. So you said that Iran wants to draw the US in for a ground war. Why would they want that? Why would they not want to avoid the war entirely and survive? Right. Um well, I mean, we have to remember that Iran is first and foremost a theocracy. They have a religious worldview. They have an esquetology. Um they're there for a reason. And in the Zoroastrian framework, there will be a final war between good and evil. And in the understanding of things, well, the United States definitely represents um evil and Iran represents good. So, they want that final conflict. It it is in their historical religious tradition to want a conflict and to triumph in the end. And that's why um Persia has been one of the most brilliations in human history. I mean they're they're not just going to sit back and and and wait and wait it out. So that's the first thing I will say. Second thing um I will say is right now Iran is being economically strangled by the United States, right? It can't really afford to wait it out because if it waits it out then a new generation of Iranians who have no memory of the revolution, the much younger uh Iranians, they will probably revolt against um Iran. the the the Iranian bure theocracy. So um you know if if you wait this out then United States economic strangulation of Iran will will have its attendant effect. So um you basically need to settle this matter before the island dies. >> So you mentioned the word esquetology. I wanted to discuss this a little later but we might as well discuss it now. in your recent video uh either you posted it like most recently or a couple videos ago, but you talked about the convergence of these different end of times esqueologgical worldviews. I'll have you describe esquetology to the audience because I I can't I don't think I could do the definition justice. Uh but if I understood you correctly, the idea is that you have these power very powerful end of times beliefs for these major civilizations and they're converging on one point. And this point of singularity is basically an all-out war, a World War II doomsday level type event uh that could reshape civilization if not destroy it entirely. Um and everyone sort of has a vested interest in escalating to that point. uh of course in a contained manner like you've described Russia uh their esqueological beliefs kind of view it as trying to rebirth the Roman Empire by retaking constant Constantinople or modern-day Istanbul that's very contained but obviously that sort of action even if it is narrow politically on the on the globe still fits into a larger framework and can suck in other powers and create a world war and then obviously you know you you talked about Jewish esquetology etc etc could you uh briefly elaborate on that to tie tie that point into what we're discussing now, how Iran, you know, in in the Zor Zoroastrian framework as you described, wants to bait the United States in and potentially escalate to a major war, >> right? Um, so esquetology, um, esquetology comes from the Greek esqueon, which means the end. So, esquetology literally means the study, um, of the end times, of how the world ends. Um, esquetology is more commonly used as to mean the understanding of how the world might end. And um, most people understand esquetology as prophecy. Uh, you know, they're trying to see how the world might end, what events might compel the um, end times. But if you see esquetology, how it's implemented in real life, it's more like an action plan. It's more of like a strategic plan on how different religious groups can, um, create the outcome that they want for their different different religious faction. And so, um, what I argue in my videos is that esquetology is a prime mover of geopolitics. Now, a lot of people will say that's incorrect. It's empire that uses esquetology in order to advance its own material interests. Um, but I disagree. I I I I think it's reverse. I think that empires come about in order to achieve certain esquetologies that and that was true for the British Empire and that's also true for the American empire. The British Empire has something called the Christian Zionist um esquetology which is trying to provoke the second coming of Jesus. Um so different traditions, different nations have their different esquetologies. And so what this is important is when you have an esquetology, you're motivated to act geopolitically. Um if you don't have an esque esquetology, like for for example, China, China doesn't have an esquetology. So for thousands of years, China has basically been content to stay within its borders and not interfere geopolitically. China has absolutely no geopolitical strategy. China has is not interested in the world except maybe trading with the world. But if it doesn't trigger the world, it doesn't really care either. So esquetology is important because it drives uh geopolitics. And what I argue is that if you look at the different esquetological traditions, uh there's at there are certain points in which they all agree in which their esquetologies converge, which means that they're working towards similar goals. And you have different powerful groups in Russia, in the United States, in Britain, in Israel, in Iran, all working towards the same goal, the same geopolitical uh framework, then chances are that it will be achieved at at at a certain point. And one of these major convergence points is a massive world war in both the Middle East and Anatolia where Turkey is based.
